title: Spike: Phase 1 - backend packaging boundary yes/no
close_reason: YES: Phase 1 architecture is packaging-compatible if it proves the managed-backend seam now and defers full unzip-and-run packaging UAT to Phase 4.

title: Spike: Phase 1 - Tauri sidecar lifecycle yes/no
close_reason: YES: Phase 1 can implement app-managed lifecycle if it proves dev Python runtime now and defers final packaged sidecar proof to Phase 4.

title: Windows Desktop Downloader UI
description:
## Feature Context

Build the first Windows desktop app experience for the existing Douyin downloader.

Locked decisions from CONTEXT.md: D1 desktop app, D15 app-managed backend, D16 portable first distribution, D2 single-link flow, D8 full batch in first version, D9 Single/Batch equal weight, D17 first UI scope limited to core download modes.

## Planning Artifacts

- history/windows-desktop-downloader-ui/CONTEXT.md
- history/windows-desktop-downloader-ui/discovery.md
- history/windows-desktop-downloader-ui/approach.md
- history/windows-desktop-downloader-ui/phase-plan.md
- history/windows-desktop-downloader-ui/phase-1-contract.md
- history/windows-desktop-downloader-ui/phase-1-story-map.md

## Current Planning State

Only Phase 1 is prepared for validation/execution. Do not implement later phase batch/cookie/history/portable work from this epic until their phase contracts and beads exist.
## Success Criteria

- Phase plans and current-phase artifacts remain traceable from feature to phase to story to bead.
- Only the approved current phase is prepared for validation and execution.
- Later phases are not implemented until their own planning/validation gates are complete.

title: Phase 1 / Story 1: Scaffold the desktop app shell and test harness
description:
## Phase Context

Phase: Phase 1 - Desktop app starts one download
What Changes: The user can open a Windows desktop app, wait for a managed backend to become ready, run one single-link download, and open the output folder.
Unlocks Next: Phase 2 batch queue can reuse the desktop shell, status layout, and backend client foundations.

## Story Context

Story: Story 1 - App shell and backend readiness
What Happens: The wrapper repo becomes a real desktop app project with a restrained Windows utility layout and test harness.
Contributes To: The phase exit state that a Tauri Windows desktop app scaffold exists and is not a browser-localhost page.
Unlocks: Backend lifecycle/readiness work can attach to a real shell and tested app state.

## Planning Context

From approach.md: Build a Tauri v2 + React + TypeScript desktop app in douyin-downloader-app and keep the UI as a practical, status-forward Windows utility.

## Institutional Learnings

No prior institutional learnings were available for this feature.

## File Scope

- package.json
- vite.config.ts
- tsconfig.json
- src/main.tsx
- src/app/App.tsx
- src/components/* only for initial shell components
- src/styles/app.css
- src/tests/* for scaffold/app-shell tests
- src-tauri/* only enough to create a runnable Tauri shell

## Task

Create the minimal Tauri v2 + React + TypeScript + Vite scaffold needed for the desktop app. The first screen should be the actual downloader workspace shell, not a landing page. Include visible Single and Batch mode placement according to D9, but implement only the Phase 1 Single path behavior later.

## Acceptance Criteria

- `npm run test` or equivalent scaffold tests pass.
- `npm run build` passes.
- `npm run tauri dev` can open a desktop app window in development.
- UI shell visually matches a clean Windows utility, not a media-heavy page or developer dashboard.
- No backend functionality is faked in production code beyond clear test doubles.
notes: MSVC linker blocker resolved. Resume bead to fix remaining scaffold gaps: tauri package metadata and src-tauri/icons/icon.ico so tauri dev can launch.
close_reason: Completed: fixed tauri scaffold blockers (package metadata + icon) and verified tauri dev launch

title: Phase 1 / Story 1: Implement backend lifecycle readiness and diagnostics
description:
## Phase Context

Phase: Phase 1 - Desktop app starts one download
What Changes: The app owns backend startup/readiness instead of requiring the user to run a terminal command.
Unlocks Next: Single URL submission can rely on a ready local backend.

## Story Context

Story: Story 1 - App shell and backend readiness
What Happens: The app starts or attaches to the managed backend, polls /api/v1/health, shows starting/ready/error state, and captures diagnostics separately.
Contributes To: The phase exit state that backend readiness comes from the app-managed process and health endpoint.
Unlocks: Story 2 can submit one URL through the real backend client.

## Planning Context

From approach.md: Use Tauri sidecar/process management and treat sidecar lifecycle as HIGH risk for validating. Do not require users to start python run.py --serve manually in the normal happy path.

## Institutional Learnings

No prior institutional learnings were available for this feature.

## File Scope

- src-tauri/src/backend.rs
- src-tauri/src/main.rs
- src/services/backendLifecycle.ts or equivalent frontend bridge
- src/components/backend readiness/status component files
- src/tests/backend lifecycle tests

## Task

Implement the app/backend lifecycle abstraction for Phase 1. It should support a test fake, a development path that can start the existing Python server or attach to a configured local process, health polling against /api/v1/health, timeout/error state, and diagnostic capture for the later Logs panel.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Tests cover starting, ready, timeout/error, and stopped/cleanup states using fakes.
- Health readiness is based on /api/v1/health, not process existence alone.
- Main UI sees only user-facing backend readiness state; raw diagnostics are kept separately.
- A dev smoke path proves the app can reach a backend without manually opening a browser UI.
- Validating has enough evidence to decide whether sidecar lifecycle needs a spike before execution.
notes: Executor clarification after validation: default Phase 1 runtime path is spawning the sibling backend in dev-python mode with the managed runtime config. Attach mode is only a configured development fallback when an explicit host/port is provided. Record smoke evidence for dev Python lifecycle; explicitly state packaged sidecar execution is deferred to Phase 4 unless separately exercised. Implement a runtime-neutral backend lifecycle abstraction with at least dev-python now and packaged-sidecar later. Dev mode may spawn sibling backend with python run.py --serve --serve-host 127.0.0.1 --serve-port <port> --config <managed-config> --path <absolute-output-folder>. Readiness must poll GET /api/v1/health, not process existence. Capture stdout/stderr diagnostics separately. Handle spawn failure, early exit, health timeout, port conflict/wrong process, and cleanup. Cleanup must terminate only the app-started process; attached external processes are left running. Tauri shell permissions must be scoped to backend command/sidecar and known args, never generic shell execution. Backend lifecycle must accept a generated runtime config path and absolute output folder before startup, and smoke evidence must record runtime mode, host/port, health response, diagnostics capture, timeout/error behavior, cleanup behavior, and whether packaged sidecar proof was deferred.
close_reason: Completed: backend lifecycle readiness now polls /api/v1/health with separate diagnostics and managed cleanup semantics

title: Phase 1 / Story 2: Add typed backend API client and config handoff
description:
## Phase Context

Phase: Phase 1 - Desktop app starts one download
What Changes: The app can submit one URL to the managed backend using the same API contract it will later reuse for batch rows.
Unlocks Next: The Single mode form can call real client functions and enter active job state.

## Story Context

Story: Story 2 - Single URL submission
What Happens: The app gets a typed client for health/download/job APIs plus a minimal output-folder/config handoff for the current app session.
Contributes To: The phase exit state that the selected output folder/config is honored when submitting one job.
Unlocks: The Single URL UI can submit a backend job and the status UI can poll by job id.

## Planning Context

From approach.md: Use internal loopback REST to the app-managed backend and write a managed config file before backend start/job submission. Current server mode is single-job and in-memory, so do not assume app history from JobManager.

## Institutional Learnings

No prior institutional learnings were available for this feature.

## File Scope

- src/services/backendClient.ts
- src/services/settingsStore.ts or Phase 1 equivalent
- src-tauri/src/settings.rs if native folder/config commands are needed
- src/tests/backend client/settings tests
- Backend repo files only if the current server cannot safely consume app-managed config/output path

## Task

Implement typed API client functions for health, create download job, get job, and list jobs. Add the minimal settings/config handoff needed for Phase 1: choose or keep an output folder and ensure the backend uses it for the submitted job. Keep persistence limited to what Phase 1 needs; full history is Phase 3.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Tests cover successful health, failed health, successful submit, failed submit, and get-job parsing.
- URL submission returns a typed job id/status object.
- Output folder/config handoff has tests proving the app-selected folder is used or serialized correctly.
- No test requires live Douyin network or real cookies.
- Any backend contract change is covered by Python server tests in the sibling backend repo.
notes: Spike constraints confirmed by .spikes/windows-desktop-downloader-ui/douyin-downloader-app-t1o/FINDINGS.md: config handoff is startup/session scoped, not a POST /api/v1/download field. Generate an app-owned runtime config outside backend config files, Tauri resources, PyInstaller extraction/resources, and bundled sidecar resources. Use absolute output paths only; default to an app-owned downloads folder under app data and create/validate it before backend start. Write config atomically using temp-write then rename. Current server deps capture FileManager(config.get(path)) at app startup, so changing output folder/config after readiness requires backend restart or submit remains blocked until readiness matches the current config version. Tests must prove generated config path and selected absolute output folder are serialized correctly, no bundled/backend config file is mutated, listJobs parses empty and active-job responses, and no test needs live Douyin/cookies.

title: Phase 1 / Story 2: Build Single mode URL submit flow
description:
## Phase Context

Phase: Phase 1 - Desktop app starts one download
What Changes: The user can paste one in-scope Douyin URL, start a job, and see the UI enter active download state.
Unlocks Next: Story 3 can poll and render the active job status.

## Story Context

Story: Story 2 - Single URL submission
What Happens: Single mode accepts one URL, handles blank/unsupported input gracefully, submits through the backend client, and records the active job id.
Contributes To: The phase exit state that Single mode can submit one URL through the backend job API.
Unlocks: Status/count UI can track the returned job id.

## Planning Context

From phase-1-contract.md: Phase 1 focuses only on one in-scope URL; full batch input/import/retry belongs to Phase 2.

## Institutional Learnings

No prior institutional learnings were available for this feature.

## File Scope

- src/components/DownloadWorkspace.tsx
- src/components/ModeTabs.tsx
- src/components/SingleDownloadForm.tsx or equivalent
- src/services/queueStore.ts only if used for the single active job state
- src/tests/single submit UI tests

## Task

Build the Single mode UI flow for Phase 1. It should include URL input, start action, disabled/loading states while backend is not ready or submit is in progress, and friendly submit errors. Keep Batch visible as a first-class tab/toggle per D9, but mark its execution as not implemented until Phase 2 without pretending batch works.

## Acceptance Criteria

- UI tests cover blank URL, disabled backend-not-ready state, submit success, and submit failure.
- Successful submit stores the active job id and moves the workspace into active-job state.
- Batch tab/toggle is visible with equal weight but does not expose fake Phase 2 behavior.
- Main screen remains clean and status-forward; no terminal log stream appears in the main download surface.
notes: Submit constraints from validation spikes: block submit until backend readiness corresponds to the current settings/config version. If the output folder changed after backend start, restart backend cleanly before POST /api/v1/download or keep submit disabled with an actionable UI state. Batch remains visible with equal weight but non-executing for Phase 1. URL handling contract: lightweight frontend validation accepts non-empty http/https URLs whose hostname is exactly douyin.com, ends with .douyin.com, is exactly iesdouyin.com, or ends with .iesdouyin.com, including short links. Reject lookalikes such as notdouyin.com, douyin.com.evil.test, evil-douyin.com, and non-http schemes. Blank input shows inline required message. Unsupported URL shows unsupported message and does not call POST. Backend unsupported RuntimeError remains handled by Story 3 fallback because backend is final authority. UI tests must cover blank URL, unsupported non-Douyin URL, lookalike host rejection, accepted Douyin URL submit, backend-not-ready disabled state, config-version mismatch disabled/restart state, submit success, and submit failure.

title: Phase 1 / Story 3: Render job polling status counts and friendly errors
description:
## Phase Context

Phase: Phase 1 - Desktop app starts one download
What Changes: The user can see what happened to a submitted single job without reading terminal output.
Unlocks Next: Phase 2 can reuse the same job status model for queue rows.

## Story Context

Story: Story 3 - Single job status and result actions
What Happens: The app polls backend job state, renders pending/running/success/failed with total/success/failed/skipped counts, and maps raw backend errors to practical friendly messages.
Contributes To: The phase exit state that the single-download loop is observable and actionable.
Unlocks: Open-folder/result actions and Phase 1 UAT proof.

## Planning Context

From approach.md: Main UI should show friendly status/errors while raw diagnostics stay in a separate diagnostics surface. Current backend errors may be raw strings like RuntimeError, so UI needs a mapping layer.

## Institutional Learnings

No prior institutional learnings were available for this feature.

## File Scope

- src/services/jobPolling.ts
- src/services/errorMapper.ts
- src/components/JobStatusPanel.tsx or equivalent
- src/tests/job polling/status/error tests

## Task

Implement job polling and status rendering for one active job. Stop polling on terminal states. Show job status, active URL/job id, and success/failed/skipped counts. Add a small error mapper so common backend failures become user-facing text while preserving raw detail for diagnostics.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Tests cover pending, running, success, failed, 404/unknown job, and polling error states.
- Polling stops on terminal states and cleans up timers/tasks.
- Counts render correctly from backend job fields.
- Friendly error text appears in the main UI; raw details are not dumped as the primary message.
- No tests require live Douyin network calls.
notes: Error mapping contract: map backend unreachable/network timeout to 'Downloader backend is not ready. Restart the app backend and try again.' with raw detail in diagnostics; 404/unknown job to 'This download job is no longer available. Start the download again.'; RuntimeError containing Unsupported URL to 'This link is not supported by the current downloader.'; cookie/auth terms such as cookie, msToken, ttwid, login, 401, 403 to 'Douyin login cookies may be missing or expired. Cookie recovery is planned for a later phase; update cookies manually for now.'; other failed job errors to 'Download failed. Check diagnostics for technical details.' Fallback must preserve raw error in diagnostics and never dump raw tracebacks as the primary main-panel message. Tests must cover each mapping plus fallback.

title: Phase 1 / Story 3: Add open output folder action and diagnostics surface
description:
## Phase Context

Phase: Phase 1 - Desktop app starts one download
What Changes: After a terminal job state, the user can take a practical next action: open the output folder used by the job.
Unlocks Next: Later history and batch result actions can reuse the same native open-folder and diagnostics patterns.

## Story Context

Story: Story 3 - Single job status and result actions
What Happens: Terminal job UI exposes an open-folder action and diagnostics remain separate from the main download surface.
Contributes To: The phase exit state that success/failure states have practical actions and logs are not streamed into the main UI.
Unlocks: Phase 2/3 result and logs surfaces.

## Planning Context

From phase-1-contract.md: Phase 1 result action may open the selected output folder; full result-history viewer and advanced logs are later phases.

## Institutional Learnings

No prior institutional learnings were available for this feature.

## File Scope

- src-tauri/src/settings.rs or native command module for opening folders
- src/components/ResultActions.tsx or equivalent
- src/components/LogsPanel.tsx only as minimal diagnostics surface if needed
- src/services/diagnosticsStore.ts if needed
- src/tests/result action/diagnostics tests

## Task

Add the native command and UI action to open the current output folder after a terminal job. Add the minimum diagnostics surface needed to prove raw backend messages are captured separately from the main workflow. Do not build full Phase 3 history/log management.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Tests or command-level checks prove open-folder is called with the selected output folder.
- The action is visible after success and available after failure when an output folder exists.
- Diagnostics/raw backend text is accessible separately and not streamed into the main download panel.
- Folder path handling is Windows-safe and handles missing/unset folder gracefully.
notes: Executor clarification: if no output folder is configured, disable the open-folder action and show a short friendly message telling the user to choose an output folder first. If the selected absolute folder no longer exists, keep raw filesystem detail in diagnostics and show a friendly main-panel error that the folder cannot be opened until it is recreated or changed. Open-folder action must use the selected absolute output folder recorded by app settings/config handoff, not infer from backend job metadata because current job responses do not include a result location. Do not shell to arbitrary paths. Diagnostics/raw backend output must remain separate from the main download panel and be available through a minimal diagnostics surface only. The action is visible after success and available after failure when an output folder exists.

title: Phase 1: Verify one desktop-managed download loop end to end
description:
## Phase Context

Phase: Phase 1 - Desktop app starts one download
What Changes: The team has proof that one desktop-managed single-download loop works or a precise blocker explaining why it cannot yet work.
Unlocks Next: Phase 2 batch planning/execution can proceed only after Phase 1 validation and execution are genuinely green.

## Story Context

Story: Phase 1 closure - prove the integrated loop
What Happens: Run the Phase 1 verification suite and a manual/dev UAT path for app launch, backend readiness, single submit, status polling, terminal state, and open-folder action.
Contributes To: The phase exit state as a whole.
Unlocks: Handoff to reviewing/next phase after execution, not before validation.

## Planning Context

From phase-1-contract.md: Demo checklist must prove desktop launch, backend health readiness, output folder, single URL submit, status/count polling, and success/failure action. Real Douyin credentials may be unavailable, so a documented fake/test backend path is acceptable for automated proof, but runtime integration must still be exercised.

## Institutional Learnings

No prior institutional learnings were available for this feature.

## File Scope

- package/test/build scripts as needed
- docs or history update only if needed for UAT notes
- no broad feature implementation files unless fixing verification gaps from prior beads

## Task

Run and document the Phase 1 verification path after implementation beads complete. This includes app tests, backend tests for any changed server contract, app build, Tauri dev/build smoke if available, and a manual/dev UAT of the single-download loop with either the real backend or an explicit fake backend when live Douyin/cookies are unavailable.

## Acceptance Criteria

- All Phase 1 automated tests pass.
- Backend tests pass if backend files changed.
- App build passes.
- UAT evidence covers desktop launch, backend readiness, one URL submit, status/count rendering, terminal state, and open-folder action.
- Any unavailable proof is reported as a blocker, not silently treated as green.
notes: Executor clarification: write or update UAT evidence at history/windows-desktop-downloader-ui/phase-1-uat.md. Phase 1 may prove dev Python backend lifecycle; do not claim portable distribution or full Phase 4 unzip-and-run release is complete. UAT notes must include command/action, expected result, actual result, evidence path/output, and blocker if unavailable. Required proof rows: desktop app launch, backend reaches /api/v1/health ready without user terminal startup, runtime mode used, host/port, health response body, diagnostics capture, cleanup behavior, generated runtime config path, selected absolute output folder, proof no runtime config/output writes occur in backend repo config/resources, one URL submit, status/count rendering, terminal state, and open-folder action. If using fake/test backend for download completion because live Douyin/cookies are unavailable, state that clearly while still proving real backend lifecycle and health. Treat PyInstaller/server-only sidecar health evidence as supporting evidence only; final packaged sidecar/release UAT remains Phase 4.

title: Spike: Phase 1 - Can Tauri manage backend sidecar lifecycle for dev and packaged-compatible runtime?
close_reason: YES: Tauri can manage backend lifecycle for Phase 1 using dev Python now and sidecar-compatible abstraction later; readiness must poll /api/v1/health and packaged mode needs scoped sidecar capabilities.

title: Spike: Phase 1 - Can backend packaging boundary support the Phase 1 architecture?
close_reason: YES: Backend packaging boundary supports the architecture if packaging uses a server-only sidecar entrypoint; naive run.py PyInstaller path is not production-ready due cli import/path and Rich banner encoding issues.

title: Spike: Phase 1 - Can output folder and config handoff be done safely for one app session?
close_reason: YES: Output folder/config handoff is safe for one app-managed backend session when app writes absolute-path runtime config before backend start and restarts backend after config changes.

title: Spike: Phase 1 - backend config handoff yes/no
close_reason: YES: Config handoff is safe only as startup/session-scoped app-owned runtime config with absolute output path; folder changes require backend restart or blocked submit.
